pr.title: calcprogress now compatible with devkitPPC r40
pr.author: camthesaxman

summary: Updated the build object naming scheme so generated object files keep the original source suffix, e.g. C sources build to `.c.o` and assembly sources build to `.s.o`. The Makefile pattern rules, linker object lists, and epilogue object list were adjusted to use those names. `tools/calcprogress.py` was also hardened to skip empty split results and ignore symbols whose object field ends in `.s.o`, making progress output consistent between devkitPPC r39 and r40.

key_files.role: Changed build pattern rules from `%.o` to `%.c.o` and `%.s.o`, and updated per-object compiler/epilogue overrides to the new names.
Updated the linker order object list so C-derived and assembly-derived objects carry `.c.o` or `.s.o` suffixes.
Updated the unscheduled epilogue object list to use the new source-suffixed object names.
Added parsing guards and skipped `.s.o` object symbols so calcprogress reports the same output under devkitPPC r39 and r40.

what_changed: Object outputs now encode their source language in the filename: `example.c` becomes `example.c.o`, and `example.s` becomes `example.s.o`.
The Makefile assembly rule changed from `$(BUILD_DIR)/%.o: %.s` to `$(BUILD_DIR)/%.s.o: %.s`.
The Makefile C rule changed from `$(BUILD_DIR)/%.o: %.c` to `$(BUILD_DIR)/%.c.o: %.c`.
Epilogue-processing rules were updated similarly, including the C epilogue rule depending on `$(BUILD_DIR)/%.c.o` instead of `$(BUILD_DIR)/%.o`.
Special-case object-specific settings such as `CC_EPI` overrides and `lbvector` CFLAGS overrides were renamed to target `.c.o` outputs.
The large linker-order list in `obj_files.mk` was mechanically renamed so assembly entries generally use `.s.o` and C entries use `.c.o`.
The epilogue unscheduled list in `e_files.mk` was renamed to the new object naming scheme.
`tools/calcprogress.py` gained a `split()`-based guard for empty symbol lines and skips lines whose final word ends with `.s.o`.
The calcprogress script file mode changed from non-executable `100644` to executable `100755`.
smart_moves: Encoding `.c` versus `.s` in object filenames gives tooling a direct way to distinguish decompiled C from remaining assembly without inferring from paths or duplicate basenames.
The Makefile rule change preserves parallel C and assembly objects with the same basename, such as cases where both `src/.../lbfile.c` and `asm/.../lbfile.s` produce linked objects.
Updating `obj_files.mk` rather than relying on implicit renaming keeps the Metrowerks linker order explicit and stable.
Updating epilogue-specific lists and overrides at the same time avoids stale references to old `.o` target names.
The calcprogress change filters by the final token ending in `.s.o`, matching the new naming convention rather than relying on older devkitPPC map formatting behavior.
Adding `len(words) == 0` before inspecting the final token makes the map parser tolerate blank or whitespace-only lines.
decomp_lessons: When build products need to serve decomp progress tooling, choose names that preserve provenance: `.c.o` means decompiled source, `.s.o` means assembly fallback.
Global object naming changes must update every consumer: compile rules, assemble rules, dependency generation, linker lists, epilogue-processing rules, and per-object flag overrides.
Progress scripts should classify objects from stable naming conventions instead of fragile assumptions about map file formatting across toolchain versions.
Compatibility with devkitPPC versions can require normalizing or ignoring map output differences rather than changing matching code.
For mixed C/ASM decomp projects, avoiding plain `basename.o` reduces ambiguity when the same logical file has both source and assembly contributions.

Mechanical renames in linker-order files are high-risk because a single stale object name can break builds or progress accounting; future similar changes should be checked with generated lists or grep-based validation.

naming_conventions.evidence: PR body explicitly gives `example.c` -> `example.c.o`; Makefile changes `%.c.o: %.c`.
PR body explicitly gives `example.s` -> `example.s.o`; Makefile changes `%.s.o: %.s`.

naming_conventions.convention: Progress tooling treats `.s.o` as assembly/not decompiled
naming_conventions.pattern: Skip calcprogress map symbols whose final split token ends with `.s.o`.

assembly_or_matching_tactics: Use object filename suffixes as a decomp status signal: `.s.o` identifies assembly-backed code and `.c.o` identifies C-backed code.
Keep both assembly and C objects in linker order when both exist for a module, but make them distinguishable by `.s.o` versus `.c.o` to avoid basename collisions.
For progress calculation, discard symbols belonging to `.s.o` objects so remaining counted symbols reflect decompiled C progress rather than assembly stubs.
The PR is primarily tooling/build infrastructure; it does not provide evidence of new function-level matching tricks or symbol renames.

review_feedback.detail: The PR context contains zero issue comments, zero review comments, and zero reviews, so no reviewer guidance or requested changes are available.

follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that rely on `.c.o` and `.s.o` naming in calcprogress or build scripts.
Search for stale plain `.o` object references after PR #303, especially in `obj_files.mk`, `e_files.mk`, and Makefile target-specific overrides.
Investigate whether any exceptions to the `.c.o`/`.s.o` convention remained intentionally, such as object names visible in the diff excerpt that did not receive a source suffix.
Look for subsequent devkitPPC r40 compatibility fixes in map parsing, linker output handling, or progress scripts.
